1: ὁ μὴ εἰσερχόμενος διὰ τῆς θύρας εἰς τὴν αὐλήν — "the one not entering through the gate into the sheep pen." Aulē (G833) — a courtyard or enclosure, not the sheep themselves (that is poimnē). The doubled amen-formula (ἀμὴν ἀμὴν) introduces the figure with the weight it carries throughout John (cf. 1:51; 3:3, 5, 11; 5:19, etc.). The scene is set at night, in a culture where sheep were gathered into a walled enclosure guarded by a single gate. The scenario is immediately legible to a Palestinian audience.
2: ὁ δὲ εἰσερχόμενος διὰ τῆς θύρας ποιμήν ἐστιν τῶν προβάτων — "the one entering through the gate is the shepherd of the sheep." The contrast is absolute: the thief/robber climbs over; the shepherd enters by the gate. The legitimacy of the shepherd's access is visible in the method of entry. No comment is made yet about who the shepherd is; the figure is presented in terms of recognizable Palestinian shepherd practice before its allegorical key is given.
3: τούτῳ ὁ θυρωρὸς ἀνοίγει — "to this one the gatekeeper opens." The gatekeeper (thyrōros, G2377) is a detail in the figure — he recognizes the legitimate shepherd and gives access. τὰ ἴδια πρόβατα φωνεῖ κατ᾽ ὄνομα — "he calls his own sheep by name." Kat᾽ onoma ("by name") signals individual, personal knowledge: not a collective summons but individual recognition. ἐξάγει αὐτά — present, ongoing leading out. The Eastern shepherd leads from the front, not driving from behind — a cultural detail that gives v4 its point.
4: ἔμπροσθεν αὐτῶν πορεύεται — "he goes before them." The shepherd precedes the flock — he does not drive them. τὰ πρόβατα αὐτῷ ἀκολουθεῖ — "the sheep follow him." Akoloutheō (G190, to follow) is the technical Johannine word for discipleship. The reason for following is specified: ὅτι οἴδασιν τὴν φωνὴν αὐτοῦ — "because they know his voice." The basis of following is recognition, and the basis of recognition is knowing (perfect participle oida, G1492).
5: ἀλλοτρίῳ δὲ οὐ μὴ ἀκολουθήσουσιν — "a stranger they will absolutely not follow." The double negative ou mē with the future indicative is the Greek idiom for the strongest possible denial. Allotrios (G245) — "belonging to another, foreign, alien" — the stranger is not merely unknown but categorically other. φεύξονται ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ — "they will flee from him." The sheep recognize the stranger's voice as not their shepherd's and respond with flight. The pastoral realism is exact: ancient shepherds had personal voices that their flocks recognized.
6: ταύτην τὴν παροιμίαν — "this figure of speech." Paroiimia (G3942) — the Evangelist's term for figurative/proverbial speech — is distinct from the synoptic parabolē. While a parable typically presents a narrative scenario for comparison, paroiimia in John tends toward a riddle or enigmatic saying that conceals meaning from some and reveals it to others (cf. 16:25, 29). ἐκεῖνοι δὲ οὐκ ἔγνωσαν τίνα ἦν ἃ ἐλάλει αὐτοῖς — "but they did not know what it was he was saying to them." The failure to understand sets up the interpretive exposition that follows.
7: ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ θύρα τῶν προβάτων — "I am the gate for the sheep." The I-AM formula with the predicate thura (G2374) — gate/door. In the figure, the gate was how legitimate shepherds entered; now Jesus identifies himself as the gate itself, through which both shepherds and sheep must pass. The claim shifts: Jesus is not only the shepherd (v11, 14) but also the sole point of legitimate access. The double amen-formula reintroduces the self-disclosure with the weight of a solemn declaration.
8: πάντες ὅσοι ἦλθον πρὸ ἐμοῦ κλέπται εἰσὶν καὶ λῃσταί — "all who came before me are thieves and robbers." The statement is absolute but contextually bounded by the immediate discourse: "all who came before me" in the role of gate (i.e., those who claimed to be the sole legitimate access to God's people) are thieves and robbers. It is not a blanket dismissal of the OT prophets but a polemic against those who positioned themselves as gatekeepers of Israel and exploited the flock. οὐκ ἤκουσαν αὐτῶν τὰ πρόβατα — "the sheep did not listen to them" — the true sheep discerned the false shepherd.
9: ἐγώ εἰμι ἡ θύρα — repeated I-AM: "I am the gate." The entry clause that follows is comprehensive: εἰσελεύσεται καὶ ἐξελεύσεται καὶ νομὴν εὑρήσει — "he will come in and go out and find pasture." Three future indicatives: entry (security in the enclosure), exit (freedom to move), and finding pasture (provision). Nomē (G3542) — pasture, the essential provision — appears only here and in 2 Tim 2:17 in the NT. Access through Jesus is not confinement but the combination of security and freedom.
10: ὁ κλέπτης οὐκ ἔρχεται εἰ μὴ ἵνα κλέψῃ καὶ θύσῃ καὶ ἀπολέσῃ — "the thief comes only to steal and slaughter and destroy." Three aorist subjunctives in purpose clauses: the thief's entire agenda is defined by what he takes and destroys. ἐγὼ ἦλθον ἵνα ζωὴν ἔχωσιν καὶ περισσὸν ἔχωσιν — "I came that they may have life and have it abundantly." Perissos (G4053) — "excess, superabundance, overflowing measure." The contrast is total: the thief's purpose is pure subtraction; Jesus's is life beyond what sufficiency alone would provide.
11: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ποιμὴν ὁ καλός — "I am the good shepherd." Kalos (G2570) — not merely "good" in a moral sense but "excellent, beautiful, noble" — the word carries both ethical and aesthetic force. The Greek phrase uses two definite articles (the shepherd, the excellent one) — this is a specific identity claim, not a generic description. τὴν ψυχὴν αὐτοῦ τίθησιν ὑπὲρ τῶν προβάτων — "lays down his life for the sheep." Tithēmi (G5087, present indicative — the laying down is his characteristic action) is deliberate: to place, to set down. Dying is here an act of will, not loss or defeat.
12: ὁ μισθωτὸς (G3411) — "the hired hand." The contrast with the shepherd turns on ownership and stake: οὗ οὐκ ἔστιν τὰ πρόβατα ἴδια — "whose own the sheep are not." Idia (own, belonging to oneself) is the same word used in vv. 3–4 for the shepherd's own sheep. The hired hand has no personal investment in the flock. When the wolf comes (erchetai ho lykos, present — it comes as surely as it must), the hired hand abandons the sheep (aphiēsin, G863) and flees. The sheep are scattered (diaskorpizei, G4650 — "scattered/dispersed").
13: ὅτι μισθωτός ἐστιν καὶ οὐ μέλει αὐτῷ περὶ τῶν προβάτων — "because he is a hired hand and does not care for the sheep." Melei autō (G3199) — "it matters to him, he cares." The negative ou melei — the sheep simply do not concern him at the level that would override self-preservation. The hired hand's flight is not cowardice; it is the logical consequence of his non-ownership. The lesson for leadership is blunt: those who serve for wages rather than vocation cannot be trusted in crisis.
14: ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ ποιμὴν ὁ καλός, καὶ γινώσκω τὰ ἐμά — "I am the good shepherd, and I know my own." The I-AM formula is repeated (cf. v11) to anchor the mutual-knowing claim that follows. Ginōskō (G1097) here is not merely cognitive recognition but the relational, experiential knowing characteristic of covenant relationship — the same verb will be used in v15 for the Father-Son mutual knowledge.
15: καθὼς γινώσκει με ὁ πατὴρ κἀγὼ γινώσκω τὸν πατέρα — "as the Father knows me, and I know the Father." The shepherd-sheep knowledge in v14 is grounded in the Father-Son knowledge in v15 — the same quality of relational knowledge (ginōskō) flows from the divine relationship into the shepherd's relationship with his flock. τὴν ψυχήν μου τίθημι ὑπὲρ τῶν προβάτων — repeated from v11 — the laying-down of life is the expression of this knowing-love, precisely as the Son gives himself in union with the Father's will.
16: ἄλλα πρόβατα ἔχω ἃ οὐκ ἔστιν ἐκ τῆς αὐλῆς ταύτης — "I have other sheep that are not of this fold." Aulē (G833, enclosure/fold) — not poimnē (flock). The "other sheep" are outside the current enclosure (the Jewish community) but still the shepherd's own. The scope is Gentile inclusion. μίαν ποίμνην, εἷς ποιμήν — "one flock, one shepherd." The final state: a single poimnē (flock), not separate pens. The structural unity runs through a single shepherd, not through a single enclosure — mission, not ethnicity, defines belonging.
17: διὰ τοῦτο ὁ πατήρ με ἀγαπᾷ ὅτι ἐγὼ τίθημι τὴν ψυχήν μου ἵνα πάλιν λάβω αὐτήν — "for this reason the Father loves me, because I lay down my life in order that I may take it up again." The Father's love (agapā, present — ongoing) is grounded in the Son's action of self-giving. Crucially, the purpose clause hina palin labō ("that I may take it up again") makes resurrection the built-in goal of the death itself — not an afterthought or reversal but the design of the laying-down.
18: οὐδεὶς αἴρει αὐτὴν ἀπ᾽ ἐμοῦ, ἀλλ᾽ ἐγὼ τίθημι αὐτὴν ἀπ᾽ ἐμαυτοῦ — "no one takes it from me; I lay it down of my own accord." The agency is entirely the Son's: no one takes, he places. ἐξουσίαν ἔχω θεῖναι αὐτήν, καὶ ἐξουσίαν ἔχω πάλιν λαβεῖν αὐτήν — "I have authority to lay it down, and I have authority to take it up again." Exousia (G1849) — delegated right, authorized power — appears twice: once for death, once for resurrection. Both are acts of authority, not compulsion. ταύτην τὴν ἐντολὴν ἔλαβον παρὰ τοῦ πατρός μου — the authority is itself received from the Father: the Son's autonomy over death is a commissioned autonomy.
19: σχίσμα πάλιν ἐγένετο ἐν τοῖς Ἰουδαίοις — "a division again arose among the Jews." Schisma palin — "division again" (G4978, G3825). The word palin links to 9:16, where the healing of the blind man produced the first schisma. The pattern is structural in John: Jesus's works and words sort listeners. διὰ τοὺς λόγους τούτους — specifically "because of these words" — the shepherd discourse is what divides them.
20: δαιμόνιον ἔχει καὶ μαίνεται — "he has a demon and is raving." Mainomai (G3105, to be mad/raving) — the charge is that Jesus is demon-possessed and insane, and therefore not worth listening to. Ti autou akouete — "why do you listen to him?" The dismissal is total. That serious people disagree about whether Jesus is insane or not is itself a Johannine sign of division, not merely a note about audience reaction.
21: ταῦτα τὰ ῥήματα οὐκ ἔστιν δαιμονιζομένου — "these words are not [the words] of a demon-possessed man." The counter-argument appeals to the quality of the teaching itself (rhēmata — spoken words/sayings). The second appeal reaches back to chapter 9: μὴ δαιμόνιον δύναται τυφλῶν ὀφθαλμοὺς ἀνοῖξαι; — "a demon cannot open the eyes of the blind, can it?" The healing stands as the test of the claim. The schism resolves into two fundamentally different readings of the evidence.
22: Ἐγκαίνια (G1456) — "the Festival of Dedication." Enkainia from kainos (new) + the dedicatory prefix — it is the feast of the rededication of the Temple after Antiochus IV Epiphanes desecrated it in 168 BC, celebrated by the Maccabees in 165 BC (1 Macc 4:52–59; 2 Macc 10:1–8). Today this is Hanukkah. The thematic resonance is deliberate: at the feast commemorating the Temple's rededication, Jesus will describe himself as the one the Father has sanctified (hēgiasen, v36 — same root as enkainia). χειμὼν ἦν — "it was winter" — a detail fixing the calendar (late November/December) and perhaps underscoring the cold reception Jesus is about to receive.
23: ἐν τῇ στοᾷ τοῦ Σολομῶνος — "in Solomon's Portico." This was the eastern colonnade of the Temple complex, known from Josephus (Ant. 20.9.7; B.J. 5.5.1–2) as a surviving structure from the Solomonic era or built to recall it. It provided shelter from wind in winter — which explains Jesus walking there in the cold month. The location will recur in Acts 3:11 and 5:12 as a gathering place for the early community.
24: ἕως πότε τὴν ψυχὴν ἡμῶν αἴρεις; — "How long will you hold our souls in suspense?" The idiom airō tēn psychēn (G142 + G5590) means to keep someone in a state of suspense or anticipation — to lift the soul up in expectation without resolution (cf. LXX Ps 24:1; 85:4). The demand that follows — εἰ σὺ εἶ ὁ Χριστός, εἰπὲ ἡμῖν παρρησίᾳ — "if you are the Messiah, tell us plainly" — uses parrēsia (G3954, boldness/open speech). The request for plain speech is ironic: Jesus has been speaking plainly; they have not received it.
25: εἶπον ὑμῖν καὶ οὐ πιστεύετε — "I told you and you do not believe." The verb tenses are precise: eipon (aorist, I told — a completed past act) vs. pisteuete (present, you do not believe — an ongoing state). The problem is not lack of information but lack of receptivity. τὰ ἔργα ἃ ἐγὼ ποιῶ ἐν τῷ ὀνόματι τοῦ πατρός μου, ταῦτα μαρτυρεῖ περὶ ἐμοῦ — "the works I do in my Father's name, these testify about me." The works-as-witness motif from 5:36 reappears: Jesus appeals not to claims but to deeds, which are the Father's testimony.
26: ἀλλὰ ὑμεῖς οὐ πιστεύετε, ὅτι οὐκ ἐστὲ ἐκ τῶν προβάτων τῶν ἐμῶν — "but you do not believe, because you are not of my sheep." The causal relation here is striking: they do not believe because they are not his sheep — not: they are not his sheep because they do not believe. Their unbelief is diagnostic, not constitutive. The sheep-belonging precedes and explains the believing, in the same way that the Father's giving precedes the coming to Jesus in 6:37–44.
27: τὰ πρόβατά μου τῆς φωνῆς μου ἀκούουσιν — "my sheep hear my voice." All three verbs are present indicative: akouousin (hear), ginōskō (I know), akolouthiousin (they follow). The present tense marks the ongoing, characteristic relationship between shepherd and sheep — not a one-time event but the habitual pattern. The hearing-knowing-following triad is the complete description of what discipleship looks like from the shepherd's perspective.
28: κἀγὼ ζωὴν αἰώνιον δίδωμι αὐτοῖς, καὶ οὐ μὴ ἀπόλωνται εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα — "and I give them eternal life, and they will never perish forever." Ou mē with the aorist subjunctive — the strongest Greek negative: absolutely not at any point. Eis ton aiōna ("for the age/forever") reinforces the temporal absoluteness. καὶ οὐχ ἁρπάσει τις αὐτὰ ἐκ τῆς χειρός μου — "no one will snatch them from my hand." Harpazō (G726, to seize by force, snatch) — the word used for violent taking. The security is not the sheep's grip on the shepherd but the shepherd's grip on them.
29: ὁ πατήρ μου ὃ δέδωκέν μοι πάντων μεῖζόν ἐστιν — "my Father, who has given them to me, is greater than all." The perfect dedōken ("has given and the giving stands") marks the Father's gift of the sheep as a completed, permanent delegation. καὶ οὐδεὶς δύναται ἁρπάζειν ἐκ τῆς χειρὸς τοῦ πατρός — "and no one is able to snatch from the Father's hand." Two hands hold the sheep: the Son's (v28) and the Father's (v29). The double security is grounded in the Father's superior greatness — nothing surpasses the Father's power, therefore nothing can overcome his hold.
30: ἐγὼ καὶ ὁ πατὴρ ἕν ἐσμεν — "I and the Father are one." The word order places the two subjects before the verb. Hen is neuter (one thing, one unity) — not heis (masculine, one person). The grammar of this sentence preserves both the reality of unity (one) and the reality of distinction (I and the Father — two subjects, plural verb esmen). The statement is not modalism (the Father and Son are the same person) but the assertion of a unity of will, purpose, action, and nature that the leaders immediately interpret as a claim to divinity (v33: "you, being a man, make yourself God").
31: ἐβάστασαν πάλιν λίθους — "they picked up stones again." Palin — "again" — referring back to 8:59. The act of stone-picking is immediate and wordless; the leaders understand the claim of v30 without needing further clarification. The use of bastazō (G941, to carry/bear) rather than a simple "they took up" suggests the weight and deliberateness of the act: they equipped themselves for execution.
32: πολλὰ ἔργα καλὰ ἔδειξα ὑμῖν ἐκ τοῦ πατρός — "I have shown you many good works from the Father." Kalos again — the word from v11 applied to the shepherd. διὰ ποῖον αὐτῶν ἔργον ἐμὲ λιθάζετε; — "for which of them are you stoning me?" The question forces the leaders to name their actual objection. Their answer in v33 is precise: not for any work, but for the claim about himself.
33: περὶ βλασφημίας, καὶ ὅτι σύ, ἄνθρωπος ὤν, ποιεῖς σεαυτὸν θεόν — "for blasphemy, because you, being a man, make yourself God." The charge of blasphemy (G988) in Jewish law applied to those who usurped divine honor or misused the divine name. The leaders' framing is exact: they understand the claim of v30 as a man claiming to be God (theon, anarthrous — they may mean "a god" or "divine"), not merely a divine agent. Whether they heard v30 as an ontological identity claim or as an improper self-elevation, the charge is precise and the stoning response is legally consistent with Lev 24:16.
34: οὐκ ἔστιν γεγραμμένον ἐν τῷ νόμῳ ὑμῶν, Ἐγώ εἶπα θεοί ἐστε; — "Is it not written in your law: I have said you are gods?" Jesus quotes the LXX of Psalm 82:6 (egō eipa theoi este). ἐν τῷ νόμῳ ὑμῶν — "in your law" — "your" is not dismissive (Jesus uses "the law" in the same way elsewhere) but addresses his opponents using their own highest authority against them. The "law" here includes the Psalms, as was common Jewish usage (cf. 12:34; 15:25).
35: εἰ ἐκείνους εἶπεν θεούς, πρὸς οὓς ὁ λόγος τοῦ θεοῦ ἐγένετο — "if he called those gods, to whom the word of God came." The argument is a qal wahomer (from the lesser to the greater): if Scripture can apply the term theos to human judges or rulers who received God's word, it cannot be blasphemy for the one who is the eternal Word to bear the title. καὶ οὐ δύναται λυθῆναι ἡ γραφή — "and Scripture cannot be broken." Lyō (G3089, to loosen, annul, dissolve) — Jesus's hermeneutical axiom: the Scripture holds. Its words, once written, carry binding force.
36: ὃν ὁ πατὴρ ἡγίασεν καὶ ἀπέστειλεν εἰς τὸν κόσμον — "whom the Father sanctified and sent into the world." Hagiazō (G37, aorist — a completed act of setting apart) resonates with the festival context of Hanukkah (Enkainia, v22 — rededication/sanctification). At the feast of the Temple's rededication, Jesus identifies himself as the one the Father truly set apart. The two aorists run in sequence: the Father first sanctified (hēgiasen), then sent (apesteilen). The sending flows from the sanctification. ὅτι εἶπον, Υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ εἰμι — "because I said, I am the Son of God" — the precise claim at issue.
37: εἰ οὐ ποιῶ τὰ ἔργα τοῦ πατρός μου, μὴ πιστεύετέ μοι — "if I do not do the works of my Father, do not believe me." The conditional makes the works the test: the claim to Sonship must be validated by the works. The works are the Father's own works performed through the Son — the origin of the works is the criterion.
38: εἰ δὲ ποιῶ, κἂν ἐμοὶ μὴ πιστεύητε, τοῖς ἔργοις πιστεύετε — "but if I do, even if you do not believe me, believe the works." This is an appeal stripped to its minimal form: if personal trust in Jesus is beyond them, the works themselves stand as evidence. The purpose clause that follows: ἵνα γνῶτε καὶ γινώσκητε — "that you may know and keep knowing." The aorist gnōte (come to know, initial knowledge) + present subjunctive ginōskete (ongoing understanding) combine: the works are meant to trigger a moment of recognition that develops into sustained understanding.
39: ἐζήτουν οὖν αὐτὸν πάλιν πιάσαι — "again they tried to seize him." Piazō (G4084) — to seize, arrest. The imperfect ezētoun ("kept seeking") marks ongoing, unsuccessful attempts. καὶ ἐξῆλθεν ἐκ τῆς χειρὸς αὐτῶν — "and he escaped their grasp." Literally, "went out from their hand." The same word for hand (cheir) used in vv. 28–29 of the Father's and Son's protective hands holding the sheep: no one can snatch the sheep from those hands; the one whose hands those are passes through the opponents' hands unharmed.
40: ὅπου ἦν Ἰωάννης τὸ πρῶτον βαπτίζων — "where John had been baptizing at first." The withdrawal to Transjordan is a return to beginnings: the place where John's ministry started (1:28, Bethany beyond the Jordan). Jesus removes himself from the Temple precincts where the confrontation has reached impasse. ἔμεινεν ἐκεῖ — "he stayed there" — menō (G3306), the Evangelist's verb of purposeful dwelling. The stay is not flight but relocation to fruitful ground.
41: Ἰωάννης μὲν σημεῖον ἐποίησεν οὐδέν — "John indeed performed no sign." The contrast is explicit: John the Baptist was a truthful witness but not a miracle-worker. His prophetic function was testimony, not demonstration. πάντα δὲ ὅσα εἶπεν Ἰωάννης περὶ τούτου ἀληθῆ ἦν — "but all that John said about this man was true." Alēthē ēn — past tense, as if summarizing a completed record: John's entire testimony has been vindicated. Toutos ("this one") — the same demonstrative the Jewish leaders used dismissively (9:29: toutou de ouk oidamen); here it marks the one about whom the truthful witness spoke.
42: καὶ ἐπίστευσαν πολλοὶ ἐκεῖ εἰς αὐτόν — "and many believed in him there." Episteusan (aorist — a decisive moment of faith). The contrast with Jerusalem is complete: where the Temple establishment attempts to stone and arrest Jesus, this mixed crowd in Transjordan (beyond the Jordan, where Gentile territory begins) believes. The first Jewish community of disciples formed around John's baptizing site; now that same ground produces faith in the one John pointed to. The Evangelist closes the unit with the simplest possible notation: many believed there.
